coredata.dc:title: Quantifying H2O2 by ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B in the presence of thioglycolic acid under the catalysis of Fe3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Xin
Yu, Xiaoxiao
Li, Benmengyang
Zhou, Rui
Fang, Qingyu
Wu, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description:
In the presence of thioglycolic acid (TGA) and under the catalysis of Fe3+, a simple, rapid, sensitive, selective and effective ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform based on the mixed physically blue nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as probe signals and orange rhodamine B as internal standard signals has been constructed for analysis of H2O2 in human serum. TGA is the key factor for fluorescence response toward H2O2 by N-GQDs and the mechanism is H2O2 reacts speedily with TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+, and produces intermediate of superoxide anions (
O2 −), which accepts electrons from N-GQDs, and generates graphene oxide, causing the fluorescence quench of N-GQDs. Compared with N-GQDs probe, the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B for analysis of H2O2 has been improved by nearly 5-folds. Under the optimum conditions, Fλ=580nm /Fλ=440nm has a good linear relationship with the concentration of H2O2 and the detection limit of H2O2 is 0.46 μmol/L with 3.5% RSD. The established sensor platform has been successfully used for probing H2O2 in human serum with satisfactory results. The superior performance of the probe lies in its high selectivity and can be directly employed in detecting H2O2 in serum samples without any sample pretreatment procedures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Catalytic
Hydrogen peroxide
Fe3+ ion
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform
Rhodamine B

Fluorescence detection of H2O2 in serum by N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform
Results and discussion
Characterization and properties of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform
Fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs single fluorescence probe
Influence of TGA on fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs
Influence of Fe3+ on fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs in the presence of TGA
Selectivity of N-GQDs fluorescence probe toward H2O2 in the presence of TGA

Fluorescence quenching type and mechanism of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
Fluorescence quenching type of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
Verification the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
Fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
Influence of pH on N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform system
Analytical assay of H2O2 by N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
Actual sample analysis
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgements

References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-GQDs, rhodamine B and N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform. (a) TEM images of N-GQDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs (red line) and CA (black line); (c) Zeta potential of N-GQDs, rhodamine B and N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform; Fluorescence spectra of rhodamine B (d), N-GQDs (e) and fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs/rhodamine B probe at the same concentration (f). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Influence of TGA and Fe3+ on probing H2O2 by N-GQDs. (a) Fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs (1), N-GQDs + TGA (2), N-GQDs + H2O2 (3) and N-GQDs + TGA + H2O2 (4). (b) Effect of with (red line) and without Fe3+ (blue line) on the response time towards H2O2 of N-GQDs in the presence of TGA. (c) Fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs (1), N-GQDs + Fe3+ (2), N-GQDs + Fe3++TGA (3), N-GQDs + TGA + H2O2 (4) and N-GQDs + Fe3++TGA + H2O2 (5). (d) Selectivity of N-GQDs probe system toward various metals ions and amino acid. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-GQDs (red line) and N-GQDs in the presence of TGA with the addition of H2O2 (black line) under excitation at 340 nm. (b) Stern-Volmer curves (F0 -F)/F0 of 8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1N-GQDs interaction with different concentration of H2O2 in B-R buffer (pH 6.0) at 293 K, 303 K and 313 K for 10 min, respectively. (c-f) are the TEM characterization of N-GQDs (c), N-GQDs + H2O2 (d), N-GQDs + TGA (e) and N-GQDs + TGA + H2O2 (f). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of the procedure for the formation of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform and the reaction mechanism of the probe system for detecting H2O2.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform incubated with different concentrations of H2O2 (4.0–40 μmol L−1) in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+. (b) The plot of Fλ=580nm /Fλ=440nm versus the corresponding concentrations of H2O2 (4.0–40 μmol L−1). (c) Selectivity of N-GQDs/rhodamine B probe system toward various metals ions and amino acid.
Analytical results of H2O2 in human serum.

head.ce:title: Quantifying H2O2 by ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B in the presence of thioglycolic acid under the catalysis of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry
Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In the presence of thioglycolic acid (TGA) and under the catalysis of Fe3+, a simple, rapid, sensitive, selective and effective ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform based on the mixed physically blue nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as probe signals and orange rhodamine B as internal standard signals has been constructed for analysis of H2O2 in human serum. TGA is the key factor for fluorescence response toward H2O2 by N-GQDs and the mechanism is H2O2 reacts speedily with TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+, and produces intermediate of superoxide anions (
O2 −), which accepts electrons from N-GQDs, and generates graphene oxide, causing the fluorescence quench of N-GQDs. Compared with N-GQDs probe, the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B for analysis of H2O2 has been improved by nearly 5-folds. Under the optimum conditions, Fλ=580nm /Fλ=440nm has a good linear relationship with the concentration of H2O2 and the detection limit of H2O2 is 0.46 μmol/L with 3.5% RSD. The established sensor platform has been successfully used for probing H2O2 in human serum with satisfactory results. The superior performance of the probe lies in its high selectivity and can be directly employed in detecting H2O2 in serum samples without any sample pretreatment procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as an important representative of reactive oxygen species (ROS), has aroused widespread concern for its role in the organism·H2O2 is a by-product, which can cause redox state changes of all cells in the normal metabolic process [1], and plays an important regulatory role in the physiological process. Almost all tissue cells in the organism are exposed to a certain concentration of H2O2, including the digestive tract, respiratory tract, eyes, plasma, and so on [2]. Under normal circumstances, H2O2 is an unavoidable but unnecessary cellular metabolite in the presence of oxygen [3]. And the faster the elimination of toxic waste H2O2, the better for the cells. As the second messenger in the cell, H2O2 plays an important role in physiological responses including regulating cell proliferation, differentiation, migration and so on [4,5], and is closely related to diseases [6,7] such as tumor [8,9], Alzheimer's disease [10,11], Parkinson's disease [12,13], peripheral arterial disease [14] and diabetes [15]. Hence, establishing a new analytical technique of probeing H2O2 for disease diagnosis and treatment has attracted more and more attention.

Traditional detection analytical techniques of H2O2 mainly include titration [16], electrochemistry [17], chemiluminescence [18] and fluorescence [19]. Among them, H2O2 responsive fluorescence probe was especially payed much attentions owing to its advantages of small damage to organisms, high signal-to-noise ratio of fluorescence signals, and convenient detection. Although fluorescence probes have many advantages and practical values, so far, many fluorescence probes have been realized by the change of single-wavelength fluorescence signal, which will be affected seriously and tightly by changes of its own concentration, light source, detector drift and the complex matrix of the sample. However, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform can excellently overcome these shortcomings. Zhu et al. [20] had successfully established a functional ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of SiO2@GQDs/MPIs@Mn-ZnS QDs by sol-gel analytical technique for sensitive, selective and visual detection of sinapic acid.

In recent years, a new type of fluorescent carbon material---graphene quantum dots (GQDs), combines the excellent performance of both graphene and quantum dots and has a strong quantum confinement effect and edge effect [21,22]. It possesses advantages of excellent photoelectric properties [23,24], low toxicity [25], good biocompatibility and stability [25,26], and has displayed important application value [23,27] in fields of cell imaging [26], biochemical analysis [28,29], and photocatalysis [30]. Boonta et al. [31] synthesized a cysteine functionalized S,N-GQDs fluorescent probe for detecting Co2+. Qu et al. [32] synthesized glutathione functionalized GQDs@GSH and designed a “turn-off-on” fluorescent probe to analyze methyl parathion in acid phosphatase and pesticides. Wang et al. [33] prepared a single-layer cyclodextrin functionalized sl-GQDs fluorescence probe for analysis of Fe3+.

In order to improve the property of GQDs, oxidative researches on GQDs were also carried out [25,34–37]. It is reported that the introduction of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs can usually cause red shift of the photoluminescence and at the same time can lead to decrease of the quantum yield [25]. GQDs react with some oxidizing ions/radicals (
OH, O2 −,
O2,
O2 −) [34–36] and generate oxygen-rich graphene oxide (GO) on the surface, resulting in the weakness of the fluorescence intensity of the original GQDs [25,34,37]. In addition, different types of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs make it display different properties. For example, CO or COOH on the surface can be used as catalase mimics [25]. Under the catalysis of Fe3+, a quick redox reaction between thioglycolic acid (TGA) and H2O2 occur and generate oxidizing intermediate of superoxide anions (
O2 −) [35,36], which may interact with nitrogen-doped graphene (N-GQDs), and its interaction with N-GQDs has not been reported yet.

In this work, a one-step hydrothermal method was employed to prepare N-GQDs using citric acid as precursor and ethylenediamine as reducing agent. Study on the interaction between N-GQDs and
O2 −, which is the oxidizing intermediate of H2O2 and TGA under Fe3+ catalysis, was performed. Based on the principle, a good selective and high ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B for rapid detecting H2O2 under Fe3+ catalysis was attempted to constructed by mixing physically N-GQDs and rhodamine B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials and instruments
ce:section.ce:para: Materials: All chemicals and biological reagents used in this work were obtained from commercial suppliers and without further purification. Analytical grade of citric acid (CA) (Tianjin Henxing, China), thioglycolic acid (TGA) (Alfa Aesar), Fe(NO3)3, rhodamine B, ethylene diamine (EDA) (Tianjin Beilian, China), H2O2 (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd), deionized ultrapure water were used. Ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), Cystine (Cys-Cys), histidine (His), tyrosine (Tyr), aspartic acid (Asp), tryptophan (Try) and NaHS were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Instruments: F-4700 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan), U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan), Nicolet 5700 infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet, America), FLS 980 time-resolved spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain), FEI field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, America) and zeta potentiometer (ZS3600 Malvern, Britain) were employed to identify N-GQDs, respectively. A PB-10 exact digital pH meter (Sartorius, Germany) was used to adjust the pH values of the aqueous solutions. Small autoclave (Fuzhou KeleishiTest Equipment Co., Ltd.) and blast drying oven (Boxun Technology Co., Ltd.) were used to prepare N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly to literature [38], N-GQDs was prepared with a one-step hydrothermal method by using CA as precursor and EDA as reducing agent. 0.21 g CA (1 mmol) and 0.18 g EDA (3 mmol) were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water, ultrasonunded for 5 min to mix well. Then the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL teflon lined stainless autoclave, sealed and heated to 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulted material was filtered with a 0.22 μm water-based filter. The yellow filtrate was collected and stored at 4 °C for later use. The concentration of the resulted N-GQDs is 0.20 mmol L−1, which is evaluated and calculated based on the amount of the original carbon source [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform was prepared simply by physically mixing 8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1N-GQDs, 1.6 × 10−5 mol L−1 rhodamine B and 5 m mol L−1B-R buffer solution (pH 6.0) at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs single fluorescence probe in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: A series of H2O2 were respectively incubated with 8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1N-GQDs, 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 TGA and 6.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 Fe(NO3)3 in 5 mmol/L B-R buffer solution at pH 6.0. The fluorescence spectra of all samples were measured at 440 nm by fluorescence spectrophotometer under 365 nm excitation after 10 min incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: A series of H2O2 (0.0, 4 × 10−6, 8 × 10−6, 1.2 × 10−5, 1.6 × 10−5, 2.0 × 10−5, 2.4 × 10−5, 2.8 × 10−5, 3.2 × 10−5, 3.6 × 10−5 and 4.0 × 10−5 mol L−1) were respectively incubated with N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform solution, 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 TGA and 6.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 Fe(NO3)3 in 5 mmol L−1B-R buffer solution at pH 6.0 for 10 min. The spectra of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of TGA/N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform were recorded by a F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer at λex = 365 nm both for N-GQDs (λem = 440 nm) and rhodamine B (λem = 580 nm), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of H2O2 in serum by N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples provided voluntarily by patients in the Laboratory Department of Huangshi Second Hospital were extracted by high-speed centrifugation (3500 rpm) and the supernatant was collected. For the detection of H2O2 in serum, all serum samples were incubated with 8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform solution, 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 TGA, 6.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 Fe(NO3)3, and different concentrations of H2O2 (0.0, 8.0, and 16.0 μmol L−1) for 10 min, respectively. After that, all serum samples were tested to obtain the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm for N-GQDs and 580 nm for rhodamine B with the excitation both at 365 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization and properties of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the structure and properties of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/ rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform, they have been characterized by UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum, FT-IR, TEM and zeta potential.

Fig. 1 a lists the TEM characterization results of N-GQDs. In Fig. 1a, N-GQDs disperses well with a narrow size distribution with a mean diameter of 2.5 ± 0.25 nm, and a clear lattice fringe of 0.201 nm, which is similar to that of the (1120) facet of graphite [39,40], discloses that N-GQDs have a graphite nature [41] and the characterization structure is consistent with the corresponding literature, indicating that N-GQDs were successfully synthesized [38,41].

Fig. 1b is the FT-IR analysis of CA (black line) and N-GQDs (red line), respectively. As shown in Fig. 1b, N-GQDs contain the stretching vibration absorption peaks of amino group (3360 cm−1), carboxyl group (1740 cm−1) [42], aromatic ring at 2930 cm−1 for C-H and at 1380 cm−1 for -C-OH [38]. In addition, N-GQDs also have characteristic stretching vibration absorption peaks [43] of NH at 704 cm−1, CN at 1400 cm−1 and CO in CONH at 1630 cm−1. Among these peaks, both CN and CO are attributed to vibration absorption peaks of the aromatic ring skeleton [31,44]. According to the above results, it can be deducted that the surface of N-GQDs is modified with oxygen-rich (COO, COOH, OH) and nitrogen-rich (NH2 ) functional heteroatoms.

Fig. 1c is the zeta potential of N-GQDs, rhodamine B and N-GQDs/rhodamine B probe. As seen, they are −12.1, −9.96 and −8.21 mV for N-GQDs, rhodamine B and N-GQDs/rhodamine B probe, respectively, and all are negative. In addition, the potential of N-GQDs and rhodamine B are both more negative than N-GQDs/rhodamine B probe. It proves that there is no interaction between the N-GQDs and rhodamine B in N-GQDs/rhodamine B probe owing to the electrostatic repulsion [44,45].

Fig. 1 d-f are the fluorescence emission spectra of rhodamine B, N-GQDs, N-GQDs/rhodamine B, respectively. Although the optimum excitation and emission peaks are λex = 550 nm, λem = 580 nm for rhodamine B (Fig. 1d) and λex = 365 nm, λem = 440 nm for N-GQDs (Fig. 1e), both N-GQDs and rhodamine B can be excited at λex = 365 nm (Fig. 1f), and whether N-GQDs and rhodamine B are mixed or not, the shape and position of their fluorescence peaks do almost not change. In addition, the difference of emission wavelength between N-GQDs and rhodamine B is 140 nm. All of these makes the possibility of constructing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform by homogeneously mixing them [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs single fluorescence probe
Influence of TGA on fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
From the results in Fig. 2 a, it can be seen that both TGA (8.0 × 10−4 mol L−1) and H2O2 (8.0 × 10−4 mol L−1) have no effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs (8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1). However, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs is quenched obviously by adding the mixture of TGA and H2O2 at pH 6. Hence, TGA is the key factor for fluorescence response toward H2O2 by N-GQDs, As illustrated in reports [35,36], a redox reaction between TGA and H2O2 occured and generated oxidizing intermediate of superoxide anions (
O2 −), which may interact with N-GQDs and thus result in the fluorescence quench. Therefore, this phenomena provides the possibility to establish a new method for quantitative probeing H2O2 by N-GQDs with the addition of TGA.

In order to further explore the effect of TGA concentration on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs, fixing N-GQDs at 8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1 and H2O2 at 8.0 × 10−4 mol L−1, influence of TGA (0.0–8.0 × 10−4 mol L−1) on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was investigated at pH 6. As shown in Fig. S1, the quenching efficiency increases quickly with TGA from 0.0 to 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1, and reaches the maximum at 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1, then decrease gradually at 2.0 × 10−5–8.0 × 10−5 mol L−1. Therefore, 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 of TGA was selected as the optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of Fe3+ on fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs in the presence of TGA
ce:section.ce:para: According to reports [35,36], under the catalysis of Fe3+, redox reaction between TGA and H2O2 is quick. Hence, fixing TGA at 2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 and pH at 6, effect of fluorescence response time toward H2O2 of N-GQDs with and without the addition of Fe3+was studied in detail. Results of the response time without (blue line) and with (red line) Fe3+ (5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1) toward H2O2 of N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2b. It is obvious that the corresponding time is more than 75 min without Fe3+, however, with the addition of Fe3+, it takes less than 10 min, and significantly speeds up the response rate. Hence, Fe3+ has to be added and 10 min was selected as the optimum.

Furthermore, with the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs has almost no changes when there is no H2O2 in N-GQDs or TGA + N-GQDs system (Fig. 2c), illustrating Fe3+ has no interference for fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs in the present of TGA.

Since the addition of Fe3+ can speed up the above reaction system, it is necessary to optimize Fe3+concentration under the optimum conditions, and the results are showed in Fig. S2. As seen, the quenching efficiency increases slowly with the concentration of Fe3+ from 0.0 to 6.0 × 10−5 mol L−1, then attains the peak at 6.0 × 10−5 mol/L, and finally decreases gradually at 6.0 × 10−5-1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1. Therefore, 6.0 × 10−5 mol L−1 of Fe3+ was selected as the optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N-GQDs fluorescence probe toward H2O2 in the presence of TGA
ce:section.ce:para: Excellent selectivity is a priority to assay the applicable capability of the probe. Thus, selective experiment was conducted by comparing the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs at 440 nm after incubation with respective agent for 10 min under the optimal conditions. Results in Fig. 2d show only H2O2 can obvious quench the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs, while other metal ions (Mn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Zr4+, Na+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Ba2+, K+, Ca2+, Ag+, Fe3+: 40 ng mL−1) and relevant biomolecules (ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys), cystine (Cys-cys), aspartic acid (Asp), histidine (His), Glucose (Glu), tryptophan (Try), tyrosine (Tyr), glycine (Gly) and H2S: 0.80 mmol L−1) can nearly not alter the the fluorescence intensity (F 0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the initial N-GQDs after adding the above components). These imply that, in the presence of TGA, the selectivity of N-GQDs fluorescence probe toward H2O2 is good.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on N-GQDs fluorescence probe toward H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of varying pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ without (blue line) and with (red line) H2O2 were investigated using B-R buffer system, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, in the range of pH 4.0–12.0, the fluorescence intensity remains basically unchanged without the adding of H2O2. On the contrary, with the adding of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity drops sharply below pH 6.0, and remains relatively stable at pH 6.0–12.0. Comprehensive analysis shows that the degree of fluorescence quenching at pH 6.0 is the largest. Therefore, B-R buffer solution at pH 6.0 was chosen as optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical assay of H2O2 by N-GQDs fluorescence probe in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the analytical performance of N-GQDs fluorescence probe in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ for detecting H2O2 was carried out, and results are displayed in Fig. S4. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the probe decreases linearly with the increase of H2O2. The calibration curve with a liner range of 3.0–160 μmol L−1 is (F0 -F)/F0 = 0.14 + 0.0027C (R2 = 0.9980) (Fig. S4b). The limit of detection (LOD) calculated as the concentration of analyte that give rise to peak areas with three times of S/N, is as low as 2.3 μmol L−1 with 2.9% RSD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching type and mechanism of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
Fluorescence quenching type of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescence quenching type of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA, in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs (red line) and N-GQDs + TGA + H2O2 (black line) was measured, and the values are τ0 = 14.53 s and τ = 13.21 ns, respectively. τ0›τ means the fluorescence lifetime is shortened with the adding of H2O2, demonstrating a dynamic quenching process [45].

To further research the quenching process, influence of different temperature (293 K, 303 K, 313 K) on the fluorescence intensity of 8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1N-GQDs in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ was studied with the addition of H2O2 (8, 16, 32, 60, 72 μmol L−1), respectively. The results in Fig. 3b processed by Stern-Volmer equation |F0 -F|/F0 = KSV [H2O2], (F0 and F respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ before and after adding H2O2 as quenching agent, KSV represents the quenching constant.) reveal that |F0 -F|/F0 all fits well linearly with [H2O2] at temperatures of 293 K, 303 K, 313 K, respectively, and the KSV increases with the increase of temperature, indicating that a dynamic quenching type plays a dominant role in the quenching process (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-GQDs by H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the reaction mechanism of the probe system more deeply, TEM characterization was made and the results of N-GQDs (c), N-GQDs + H2O2 (d), N-GQDs + TGA (e), and N-GQDs + TGA + H2O2 (f) are shown in Fig. 3c-f respectively.

As compared Fig. 3c (N-GQDs) with Fig. 3d (N-GQDs + H2O2) and Fig. 3e (N-GQDs + TGA), the morphology of N-GQDs is not only almost no changes, but also the lattice fringe spacing (N-GQDs: 0.201 nm, N-GQDs + H2O2 and N-GQDs + TGA: 0.207 nm) are very close and nearly the same. This demonstrates that neither H2O2 not TGA interact with N-GQDs. However, by comparing Fig. 3f and Fig. 3c, with the addition of both TGA and H2O2, the morphology is different and a difference of 0.88 nm of the lattice fringe spacing exists (increasing from 0.201 to 0.289 nm). It was reported that 0.289 nm is the characteristic lattice fringe spacing of GO (graphene oxide) [25,34–37]. Hence, the mechanism is presumed that H2O2 reacts with TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+, and rapidly generates intermediate of superoxide anions (
O2 −), which further undergoes a redox reaction with N-GQDs by accepting electrons from N-GQDs, resulting in the generation of graphene oxide [25,34–37], and causing the fluorescence quench of the original N-GQDs [20,31]. Fig. S5 visually illustrates the reaction mechanism of the probe system in detail, and the relevant reaction equations are as follows.
(1)
H 2
O 2
+ H S
C H
2
C O O H
T G A
→
F e
3 +
H
O 2
· + · H S O C O
C H
3
(2)
H
O 2
→ ·
O 2
-
+ H
+
(3)
O H + · H S O C O
C H
3
→ H
S O
2
C O
C H
3
(4)
G Q D s + ·
O 2
-
→ G O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Taking into account the improvement of the accuracy and sensitivity of the strategy for probeing H2O2, based on the above study on fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs single probe in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ and by taking advantage of the response difference toward H2O2 of N-GQDs and rhodamine B in the above system, a new N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform was attempted to construct by physically mixing blue N-GQDs (as detecting signal) and orange rhodamine B (as internal standard signal) for probing H2O2. Fig. 4 illustrates the detailed formation process and mechanism for fluorescence response toward H2O2 of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+. It is clearly that, in the ratiometric sensor platform, N-GQDs evokes a response toward H2O2, while rhodamine B not, meaning rhodamine B does not take part in the redox reaction with H2O2 + TGA + Fe3+.

To further understand the interaction between N-GQDs and rhodamine B, N-GQDs and rhodamine B were mixed physically and varied one concentration by keeping the other at one level under the optimum conditions. The fluorescence response of the resulted samples were tested and the results are listed in Fig. S6. As shown, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs and rhodamine B both increase with the amount of N-GQDs and rhodamine B, while their fluorescence intensities remain unchanged when their concentrations fix at one level, indicating no interaction between N-GQDs and rhodamine B. To obtain an obvious variation, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform was constructed by physically mixing 200 μL N-GQDs and 400 μL rhodamine B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform system
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of different pH values in B-R buffer solution on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs, rhodamine B, and N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platforms were further investigated respectively. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs and rhodamine both gradually increase at pH 2.0–6.0, then reach a platform at pH = 6.0–11.0 and subsequently, show a downward trend at pH 11.0–12.0. Regardless of whether N-GQDs and rhodamine B are mixed or not, they both have the same pH effects for each of them. Strong acid or base environment is unfavorable to both N-GQDs and rhodamine B owing to the protonation and deprotonation, and resulting in the decrease of fluorescence intensity [45]. Comprehensive considering effects of pH on N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform, B-R buffer solution at pH 6.0 was chosen as optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical assay of H2O2 by N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To study the sensitivity and precision of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform, under optimal conditions, the analytical performance of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ for probeing H2O2 was tested, and results are listed in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs gradually decreases with the increase of N-GQDs, while rhodamine B keeps unchanging (Fig. 5a). The calibration curve with a liner range of 4.0–40 μmol L−1 is Fλ=580nm /Fλ=440nm = 1.05 + 0.080C (R2 = 0.9951) (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) calculated as the concentration of analyte that give rise to peak areas with three times of S/N, is as low as 0.46 μmol/L with 3.5% RSD.

The performance of probing H2O2 in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+ by N-GQDs single probe and N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform is compared in Table S2. It is obvious that the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B has been improved by nearly 5-folds (from 2.3 to 0.46 μmol L−1) in spite of the narrowing of the linear range. Therefore, the construction of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform both improves effectively the accuracy and sensitivity for probing H2O2.

H2O2 can be detected by various methods such as fluorescence method [46–49], electrochemical method [50,52], colorimetric method [51,53], and so on. Table S3 compares the analytical performance of this experiment with other reported methods for probeing H2O2. As seen, LOD of this method is lower than the studies of Liu et al. [46], Du et al. [47] and Yagati et al. [50], close to the works of Wang et al. [48] and Sui et al. [51], higher than the results of Mohammed et al. [52], Zhang et al. [53] and Liu et al. [49]. Among these methods, the complicated electrode preparation process makes the electrochemical method to be more cumbersome. In addition, compared with the single-signal fluorescence probe [44,45], N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform can use a built-in reference signal to eliminate the interference caused by the environmental signal, excitation light intensity and sample matrix, and thus possesses higher accuracy and superiority.

Selectivity of N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform system. Under the optimum conditions, Fig. 5c shows the effect of 40 ng mL−1 metal ions (Mn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Zr4+, Na+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Ba2+, K+, Ca2+, Ag+, Fe3+) and 0.80 mmol L−1 of various coexisting biomolecules that may exist in serum (ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys), cystine (Cys-cys), aspartic acid (Asp), histidine (His), Glucose (Glu), tryptophan (Try), tyrosine (Tyr), glycine (Gly) and H2S) on probing H2O2 by N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescent probe in the presence of TGA with Fe3+ catalysis. It is clear that there is no interference, implying N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric sensor platform system shows excellent selectivity toward H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Actual sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B in the presence of TGA with Fe3+ catalysis was applied to analyze H2O2 in human serum to verify the practical feasibility. Table 1 shows the results of H2O2 in four human serum samples provided by volunteers of the patients from the Clinical Laboratory in the Second Hospital of Huangshi·H2O2 found in four human serum samples by this method is 5.3, 6.0, 5.3 and 6.0 μmol L−1, respectively. The acceptable rates of 98.45%∼117.1% imply that N-GQDs/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform has good potentials for probing H2O2 in human serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para:
In this experiment, TGA added as a redox mediator into N-GQDs and H2O2 system under the catalysis of Fe3+ is the key factor for fluorescence response toward H2O2 by N-GQDs. The mechanism is that H2O2 reacts speedily with TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+, and produces intermediate of superoxide anions (
O2 −), which accepts electrons from N-GQDs, and generates graphene oxide, causing the fluorescence quench of the original N-GQDs. By mixing physically N-GQDs with rhodamine B, a new ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform has been constructed for probeing H2O2 in human serum in the presence of TGA under the catalysis of Fe3+. Compared with N-GQDs single probe, the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform of N-GQDs/rhodamine B has been improved by nearly 5-folds. The superior performance of the probe lies in its high selectivity and can be directly employed in detecting H2O2 in serum samples without any sample pretreatment procedures, and has good application prospects in the field of medicine and biochemical testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Xin Zhan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaoxiao Yu: Investigation, Resources. Benmengyang Li: Investigation, Resources. Rui Zhou: Investigation, Resources. Qingyu Fang: Investigation, Resources. Yiwei Wu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by Health Commission of Hubei Province (WJ2019H475), Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technique (Hubei Normal University) (PA200106).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary material
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121191.
